chunks.title: Biography
chunks.content: Tim Davis is a Professor of Computer Science at Texas A&M University. His primary scholarly contribution is the creation of widely-used sparse matrix algorithms and software (SuiteSparse), including most of the sparse solvers behind x=A\b in MATLAB, and many other commercial packages. For example, his codes power the semiconductor processing of all high-end semiconductor fabs. Every Apple device uses his solvers, and they are also an integral part of Pokemen Go (for augmented reality). Fellow IEEE, SIAM, ACM.

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Discrete Structures for Computing. (3-0). Credit 3. Mathematical foundations from discrete mathematics for analyzing computer algorithms, for both correctness and performance; introduction to models of computation, including finite state machines and Turing machines. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in MATH 142, MATH 147, MATH 151, or MATH 171. Cross-listed with ECEN 222.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in MATH 142, MATH 147, MATH 151, or MATH 171. Cross Listing: ECEN 222/CSCE 222.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Discrete Mathematics is to computer science as words and grammar are to English. Solving a problem in computer science without discrete math is like writing a novel with garbled grammar or a confusing plot. It just won't work. Your thinking will be muddled, and your code just won't work. Worse yet, your code will likely have hidden bugs that won't be found until an end-user complains (or explodes ...). At that point, fixing the bug is costly, or even impossible.

This course teaches you the fundamentals of logic, proof techniques, induction/recursion, counting, advanced counting (not as easy as it sounds!), relations, graph theory, models of computing, and finite state machines. These mathematical tools are essential to doing and understanding computer science / computer engineering.

The following are the formal learning outcomes of the course. Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
1. understand the basic principles of logic, proofs and sets.
2. apply results from discrete mathematics to the analysis of algorithms.
3. produce proofs by induction and apply counting techniques.
4. explain models of computation.
5. understand and apply results from graph theory, relations, and trees

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications
ISBN: 1260920240
Authors: K. Rosen
Publisher: McGraw-Hill
Publication Date: 2025
Edition: 2025 Release
URL for Resource: https://www.mheducation.com/highered/product/Discrete-Mathematics-and-Its-Applications-Rosen.html

chunks.title: Notes
chunks.content:
You must use the 2025 Release (in any format: ebook, print, etc). You cannot use the 8th (or earlier) editions. See:
https://www.mheducation.com/highered/product/discrete-mathematics-and-its-applications-rosen.html

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Exams 75%: we will have two in-class exams during the semester, and a final exam. Each is worth 25% each.

Homework: 25%. Homework is always due at EXACTLY 3:55pm on a class day, so that I can discuss the solution in class that day. So normally I cannot accept late submissions, except with prior permission. Homework is assigned, submitted, and graded on Canvas. Unless otherwise noted on the assignment, all homework is to be done by yourself. You may discuss the homework with each other but you must solve the problems yourself. When discussing with each other, pretend you are in my office discussing it with me. I will help you understand the problem but I wouldn't give you the solution. If you do discuss the homework with anyone, state their name on your submission (either on the HW itself or on a note on Canvas). You may not use Al in any way.

Grading scale (after curving of individual items, as needed):
• 90 or higher: A
• 80: B
• 70: C
• 60: D
• less than 60: F

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content: No late assignments accepted without a priori permission. Turning in a homework assignment one minute past the due date

chunks.title: Topics, with sections/chapters of the book:
chunks.content:
1 The Foundations: Logic and Proofs
1.1 Propositional Logic
1.2 Applications of Propositional Logic
1.3 Propositional Equivalences
1.4 Predicates and Quantifiers
1.5 Nested Quantifiers
1.6 Rules of Inference
1.7 Introduction to Proofs
1.8 Proof Methods and Strategy

2 Basic Structures: Sets, Functions, Sequences, Sums, and Matrices.
2.1 Sets
2.2 Set Operations
2.3 Functions
2.4 Sequences and Summations
2.6 Matrices

3 Algorithms
3.1 Algorithms
3.2 The Growth of Functions
3.3 Complexity of Algorithms

4 Number theory and cryptography
4.1 Divisibility and Modular Arithmetic
4.3 Primes and Greatest Common Divisor

5 Induction and Recursion
5.1 Mathematical Induction
5.2 Strong Induction and Well-Ordering
5.3 Recursive Definitions and Structural Induction
5.4 Recursive Algorithms

6 Counting
6.1 The Basics of Counting
6.2 The Pigeonhole Principle
6.3 Permutations and Combinations

8 Advanced Counting Techniques
8.1 Applications of Recurrence Relations
8.3 Divide-and-Conquer Algorithms and Recurrence Relations

9 Relations
9.1 Relations and Their Properties
9.3 Representing Relations
9.5 Equivalence Relations

10 Graphs
10.1 Graphs and Graph Models
10.2 Graph Terminology and Special Types of Graphs
10.3 Representing Graphs
10.4 Connectivity

11 Trees
11.1 Introduction to Trees
11.2 Applications of Trees
11.3 Tree Traversal

13 Modeling Computation
13.2 Finite-State Machines with Output
13.3 Finite-State Machines with No Output
13.4 Language Recognition
13.5 Turing Machines
